topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink bled across the final paper, a stark, unforgiving crimson marking a 'D-'. Liam stared at the grade as if it were a foreign object, too bright, too sharp. His stomach tightened into a hard knot, and he instinctively picked at a loose thread on his sleeve, a nervous habit he hadn't realized he was indulging in. He reread the feedback, the professor’s notes a cascade of critical observations that seemed to confirm every fear he’d harbored about his preparation. When Sarah walked past his desk, whistling a cheerful tune, Liam quickly shoved his laptop screen face down, hunching his shoulders inward as if trying to make himself smaller, hoping the sudden shadow of his posture would disappear.

[story 2]

"A C-minus," the automated announcement chirped from the lecture hall speakers, a sound that felt amplified in the sudden, heavy silence of the room. Maya felt a flush creep up her neck, warm and undeniable. She gripped the edge of her desk until her knuckles turned white, her gaze darting everywhere but at the professor, who was already moving on to the next topic. *This isn't right,* she thought, a frantic whisper in the echoing space. She began meticulously organizing her notes, smoothing the creases on the paper until the texture felt reassuringly flat under her fingertips, a desperate attempt to impose order on the swirling uncertainty in her chest.

[story 3]

He didn't look up when the results were posted. The number—a 68%—seemed to vibrate with disapproval. Mark chewed on the inside of his cheek, the dry taste of anxiety sharp on his tongue. Every movement he made felt exaggeratedly slow, his steps dragging across the linoleum floor. When his roommate finally knocked, Mark flinched, quickly gathering his scattered textbooks into a tight, defensive stack. "Yeah, I got it," he managed, his voice sounding unnaturally high, trying to project a casualness he absolutely did not feel. He focused intensely on the pattern of the floor tiles, counting them, anything to avoid meeting anyone's eyes.

His stomach tightened into a hard knot, and he instinctively picked at a loose thread on his sleeve, a nervous habit he hadn't realized he was indulging in. He reread the feedback, the professor’s notes a cascade of critical observations that seemed to confirm every fear he’d harbored about his preparation. When Sarah walked past his desk, whistling a cheerful tune, Liam quickly shoved his laptop screen face down, hunching his shoulders inward as if trying to make himself smaller, hoping the sudden shadow of his posture would disappear.

"A C-minus," the automated announcement chirped from the lecture hall speakers, a sound that felt amplified in the sudden, heavy silence of the room. Maya felt a flush creep up her neck, warm and undeniable. She gripped the edge of her desk until her knuckles turned white, her gaze darting everywhere but at the professor, who was already moving on to the next topic. *This isn't right,* she thought, a frantic whisper in the echoing space. She began meticulously organizing her notes, smoothing the creases on the paper until the texture felt reassuringly flat under her fingertips, a desperate attempt to impose order on the swirling uncertainty in her chest.

He didn't look up when the results were posted. The number—a 68%—seemed to vibrate with disapproval. Mark chewed on the inside of his cheek, the dry taste of anxiety sharp on his tongue. Every movement he made felt exaggeratedly slow, his steps dragging across the linoleum floor. When his roommate finally knocked, Mark flinched, quickly gathering his scattered textbooks into a tight, defensive stack. "Yeah, I got it," he managed, his voice sounding unnaturally high, trying to project a casualness he absolutely did not feel. He focused intensely on the pattern of the floor tiles, counting them, anything to avoid meeting anyone's eyes.
